cards.term: what is std::cout <<
cards.expl:
- is the standard output stream operator used to print 
  (or output) data to the console.


std   - This is the namespace where most of the C++ Standard Library symbols 
        (like cout, cin, etc.) live.

cout  - Short for “character output”. 
        It’s an object of the class std::ostream that represents 
        the standard output stream (usually your terminal or console).

<<    - This is the insertion operator. It’s used to send 
        (or "insert") data into the output stream.

cards.term:
what is this actually doing:
<< std::endl;
cards.expl:
- Insert a newline character \n
- Flushes the output buffer 
  meaning: forces the text to appear immediately on the screen/terminal

- using '\n' is faster and behaves exactly as you expect in almost every situation.

cards.term: what does "flushing" mean ?
cards.expl:
- Flushing means Forcing that buffer to empty right now — 
  sending everything it holds to the actual output device 
  (like your console or terminal).

- the text "Hello, world!\n" doesn’t always go immediately to your screen.

- Instead, C++ stores it temporarily in a buffer — 
  a small chunk of memory that holds output until it’s convenient 
  to actually display or write it.
  
  This makes I/O faster, because writing to the screen 
  (or a file) is relatively slow.

cards.term: Why people avoid std::endl
cards.expl:
- std::endl flushes the output buffer every time — that can make programs slower,   
  especially in loops or heavy output.

cards.term: When you might still want std::endl
cards.expl:
- You need to ensure the text appears immediately 
  (like before asking for user input or debugging a crash).

cards.term:
write the megaphone main:

$>./megaphone "shhhhh... I think the students are asleep..."
SHHHHH... I THINK THE STUDENTS ARE ASLEEP...

$>./megaphone Damnit " ! " "Sorry students, I thought this thing was off."
DAMNIT ! SORRY STUDENTS, I THOUGHT THIS THING WAS OFF.

$>./megaphone
* LOUD AND UNBEARABLE FEEDBACK NOISE *

cards.expl:
- This function does not modify the object it belongs to.

- If you try to modify firstName or any other member inside this function, 
  the compiler will give an error.

private:
  - Only a class itself can see or change these members
  - Example:
      firstName is private:
          you cant access it directly from outside the class
          
public:
  - Anyone can use these functions from outside the class
  - Example:
      setInfo() is public:
          main program can call it to fill a contact


+----------------+
|   Contact      |
|----------------|
| private stuff  | <- hidden inside, no direct access
| public stuff   | <- door to interact safely from outside
+----------------+

cards.term:
Whats the difference between:

std::cin >> command
std::getline(std::cin, command)
cards.expl:
std::cin >> command
    -> reads one word (stops at any space, tab, or newline).

std::getline(std::cin, command)
    -> reads the whole line, including spaces, until the user presses Enter.

cards.term: Why do we include headers like <iostream> and <string> in a .cpp file even if they are already included in the header?
cards.expl:
- Each .cpp file is compiled separately, 
  so it must include all headers it directly uses. 
  
- This makes the file self-sufficient, avoids hidden dependencies, 
  and is common C++ practice.

cards.term: Why do we include <string> but not <iostream> in a C++ header file?
cards.expl:
- Include <string> if the class uses std::string in its members or declarations. 

- <iostream> isn’t needed in the header if we only use input/output in the .cpp file, 
  keeping headers minimal and avoiding unnecessary dependencies.

cards.term: What is a constructor in C++?
cards.expl:
- A constructor is a special class function automatically called when an object is created.

- It initializes the object’s members.

- It has the same name as the class and no return type.

- Example:
    PhoneBook::PhoneBook() { count = 0; nextIndex = 0; }

cards.term: What are the two ways to write a constructor in C++ to initialize members?
what is static allocation?
What is dynamic allocation?
What is an instance of a class?

cards.expl:
Static Members
- Belong to the class, not any one object
- Shared among all instances
- Declared in the class, defined once outside it
- Often used for counters, totals, shared state

Non-static Members

- Belong to each individual object
- Initialized per object (each instance has its own value)
- Set in constructor’s initializer list or body

cards.term: where do u init static members and how often?
cards.expl:
- you initialize static members only once in a single .cpp file

- After that, any .cpp file that includes Account.hpp can use the static members, 
  but you don’t re-initialize them anywhere else.

- The part after the colon : is called an initializer list.

_accountIndex(_nbAccounts)  -> set _accountIndex to _nbAccounts.
_amount(initial_deposit)    -> set _amount to the constructor argument.
_nbDeposits(0)              -> start at 0
_nbWithdrawals(0)           -> start at 0

- It looks like a function call, 
  but it’s really just directly initializing the member variable
  before the constructor body runs.

- A static member function belongs to the class itself, not to any object.
- It can be called without an instance of the class
    Account::_displayTimestamp()
    
- It cannot access non-static members (like _amount) 
  because there’s no specific object (this) associated.
  
- _displayTimestamp() is static because it only prints the timestamp and 
  doesn’t need any data from a specific account.

- A destructor is a special class function that runs automatically
  when an object is destroyed (goes out of scope or is deleted).
  
- It has the same name as the class but with a ~ in front.

- It cleans up the object — like freeing resources, closing files, or logging info.


Key points:

  - No arguments
  - Only one per class
  - Called automatically

cards.term:
what does this do?

typedef unsigned int uint;
cards.expl:
- Now uint can be used instead of unsigned int.

- typedef creates a new name (alias) for an existing type

cards.term: What is Account::t ?

What does this mean?

typedef std::vector<Account::t> accounts_t;
What does typedef std::vector<Account::t> accounts_t; do?
cards.expl: It defines accounts_t as an alias for std::vector<Account>, so accounts_t is a vector of Account objects.

cards.term: What is a vector in C++?

cards.expl:
- accounts_t is a vector (list) that holds Account objects.

- we create 8 accounts, one for each initial deposit amount.
    -> accounts[0] → Account with amount 42
    -> accounts[1] → Account with amount 54
    -> and so on…

cards.term: What’s the difference between an array and a vector?
cards.expl:
- Array = fixed-size, low-level, can’t grow or shrink.
    - Fixed size — you can’t resize it after creation.
    - Stored on the stack (usually).

    int arr[3] = {1, 2, 3};
    arr[0] = 42;    // ok
    arr[3] = 99;    // ❌ out of range, undefined behavior
    
- Vector = is dynamic, can grow and shrink, part of the C++ Standard Library.
    - It manages its own memory automatically.
    
    std::vector<int> v = {1, 2, 3};
    v.push_back(4); // now v = {1, 2, 3, 4}
    v.pop_back();   // removes last element

cards.term:
The Account constructor takes only one argument, for the vector why we do this 
    accounts_t accounts(amounts, amounts + amounts_size);  ?
cards.expl:
- This is a special std::vector constructor.
- It can take two iterators (or pointers) — a range — 
  and automatically fills the vector with objects constructed from that range.
  
      std::vector<Type> vec(begin, end);
      
      Create a vector and fill it with elements built from 
      everything between begin and end
         
- In our case:
    - amounts is a pointer to the first int in the array.
    - amounts + amounts_size points one past the last int.
    
    
How does it know how to make an Account from an int?
    - Because the Account class has a constructor that takes one int:
        Account::Account(int initial_deposit);
    - So when we say:
        std::vector<Account> accounts(amounts, amounts + amounts_size);
    - C++ says:
        For each int in that range, call Account(int) and 
        put the result into the vector.
        
        - So internally, it’s doing something like:
            accounts.push_back(Account(42));
            accounts.push_back(Account(54));
            ...

cards.term: What’s a std::pair?
cards.expl:
- std::pair<A, B> is a simple structure that holds two values, 
  possibly of different types.

- Example:
     std::pair<int, std::string> example(42, "hello");
    - You can access them as:

        example.first;   // 42
        example.second;  // "hello"
        
- So a pair is just two things bundled together.

cards.term:
What is 

typedef std::pair<accounts_t::iterator, ints_t::iterator> acc_int_t;?
cards.expl:
- It defines acc_int_t as a pair of iterators
    - one that points to an Account in the accounts vector, 
    - and one that points to an int in the deposits (or withdrawals) vector.

- Let’s decode that.
    - accounts_t = std::vector<Account>
    - accounts_t::iterator = an iterator (a kind of pointer) 
      that can move through that vector.
    - ints_t = std::vector<int>
    - ints_t::iterator = iterator that moves through a vector of ints.

cards.term: What is iterator in general?
cards.expl:
- An iterator is like a smart pointer that allows you to move through a container     
  (like a vector, list, or map) element by element.
  
- It behaves a bit like a pointer:

    - You can access the value with *it
    - You can move to the next element with ++it
    - You can compare it to another iterator with == or !=


- Every std::vector type automatically defines an inner type called iterator.
      
      typedef std::vector<Account::t> accounts_t;
      
      accounts_t::iterator
        -> is the type of iterator 
           that can be used to walk through that vector of accounts.

cards.term: What does accounts_t::iterator mean?
cards.expl:
- accounts_t::iterator is the iterator type used to move through a 
  std::vector<Account> 
  (like a smart pointer that can step through each Account in the vector).

cards.term:
How it’s used:

typedef std::vector<Account::t> accounts_t;
